nodes.name: 橫斑林鴞
nodes.summary: 橫斑林鴞（Barred owl，学名 Strix varia），在图谱中标记为无危（LC）物种，已关联28个分布地点节点，常见栖息地包括成熟森林、稀树草原、湿地等4项。
nodes.shortSummary: 橫斑林鴞（學名：Strix varia）是鴟鴞科林鴞屬的一種大型貓頭鷹。 特徵 橫斑林鴞長44-46厘米，翼展101-115厘米。它們的面部淡色，眼睛周圍有深色的環。喙呈黃色，眼睛褐色。它們是美國東部唯一擁有褐色的眼睛的貓頭鷹，其他的都是黃色眼睛。頭部很圓，沒有耳羽。上身呈灰褐…

links.evidence: |journal=Ibis |volume=21 |issue=3 |pages=346–352 }} Barred owls are largely native to eastern North America, but have expanded their range to the west coast of North America where they are considered invasive.Evers, L

links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: thumb|Winter in Vermont The barred owl is distributed throughout most of the eastern United States, as well as much of southern Canada. They are found as far northeast as much of Nova Scotia (western two-thirds), New Brunswick and Sept-Îles, in much of Quebec, up to Lake Mistassini, and Ontario, up to Moosonee.{{cite book|author=Erskine, A

links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: Ontario Birds, 11, 18–22. The barred owl has been recorded as far north as central Labrador, though the species is not yet confirmed to breed in the province. The barred owl ranges in every part of the eastern United States continuously from northernmost Maine down throughout New England, the Mid-Atlantic states, much of the Midwest, the Southeast United States and all of Florida. Their range extends without substantial gaps to the limits of western Minnesota, easternmost South Dakota, the southeastern corner of Nebraska, the eastern half roughly of Kansas, most of Oklahoma and east Texas to as far west as Cisco and Burnet.Janssen, R

links.evidence: |journal=The Journal of Wildlife Management |date=1984 |volume=48 |issue=4 |pages=1393–1398 }} They are often found in bottomland hardwood forests in the largest swath of the native breeding range, often (particularly from Virginia south and west) with deep, dark stands of oak, gum and cypress.{{cite journal|author=Dickson, J

links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: |journal=The Journal of Wildlife Management |date=1984 |volume=48 |issue=4 |pages=1393–1398 }} They are often found in bottomland hardwood forests in the largest swath of the native breeding range, often (particularly from Virginia south and west) with deep, dark stands of oak, gum and cypress.{{cite journal|author=Dickson, J

links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: |year=2004|title=Density and habitat associations of barred owls at the edge of their range in Oklahoma|jstor=3878075|journal= Southeastern Naturalist|volume= 3|issue=3|pages= 475–482|doi= 10.1656/1528-7092(2004)003[0475:DAHAOB]2.0.CO;2 |s2cid= 85726566 }} Along the Atlantic coast area from New England to New Jersey, barred owls are often found in mixed swamps areas with cedars, seldom wandering to adjacent wetlands or farmland.{{cite journal|author1=Sutton, C

links.label: 分布于

links.label: 栖息于
links.evidence: Thesis, Oregon State University. Mature forests are their preferred habitat, but they can also acclimatise to various gradients of open woodlands.Mazur, K

links.label: 栖息于
links.evidence: |year=1988|title=Bird communities in oak-gum-cypress forests|journal= Bird Conservation|volume= 3|pages= 51–62}} Secondary habitat, often used during foraging forays in the south, are often oak savanna or cabbage-palm areas. Regardless of area, some variety of water is frequently present, including riparian areas or swampy ground.{{cite journal|url=https://biostor.org/reference/215761|author1=Laidig, K

links.label: 栖息于
links.evidence: |year=1995|title=Spatial overlap and habitat associations of barred owls and great horned owls in southern New Jersey|journal= Journal of Raptor Research|volume= 29|issue=3|pages=151–157}} Closed canopy forests were preferred in a study from Oklahoma (62.8% of habitat used), followed by fallow agricultural fields (10.6%), wetlands (8.1%) and open terrain (6.2%)

links.label: 栖息于
links.evidence: |year=2004|title=Density and habitat associations of barred owls at the edge of their range in Oklahoma|jstor=3878075|journal= Southeastern Naturalist|volume= 3|issue=3|pages= 475–482|doi= 10.1656/1528-7092(2004)003[0475:DAHAOB]2.0.CO;2 |s2cid= 85726566 }} Along the Atlantic coast area from New England to New Jersey, barred owls are often found in mixed swamps areas with cedars, seldom wandering to adjacent wetlands or farmland.{{cite journal|author1=Sutton, C
